name: Mt. Pisgah
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding site near Springfield featuring agate and jasper specimens on gentle slopes. No permit required - collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use.
description:
Mt. Pisgah is a BLM-managed public land rockhounding destination in Lane County's Springfield area, offering accessible collecting opportunities for agate, jasper, and quartz varieties. The site sits at a modest elevation of 1529 feet with gentle slope terrain that makes it suitable for rockhounders of varying skill levels. This location provides a rare combination of easy public access and productive collecting without the need for permits or fees.

The geology of this area reflects the Cascade Range foothills region, where Tertiary volcanic activity and subsequent weathering have concentrated silica-rich minerals in the soil and exposed outcrops. Agate nodules and banded jasper specimens are found scattered across the slope, often exposed in draws and erosional washes where winter rains have worked them loose from surrounding host rock.

Collectors should expect to work gently sloped terrain with mixed vegetation and clear sight lines across the landscape. The accessible nature of Mt. Pisgah makes it popular for family outings and beginners who want hands-on rockhounding experience without technical climbing or remote backcountry travel. Early morning visits often yield better surface collecting as sunlight helps highlight translucent agate specimens against the darker soil.

As BLM land, Mt. Pisgah operates under recreational use guidelines that emphasize personal collection only - this is not a commercial operation or pay-to-dig site. Respect for the landscape and adherence to the 25-pound daily limit helps preserve this resource for future rockhounders. Always fill any small holes you dig and leave the site cleaner than you found it.
history: This region sits within the Cascade Range foothills, where Miocene-era volcanic activity deposited silica-rich fluids that eventually crystallized into agate nodules and jasper deposits scattered through the host rock and weathered soils. The present-day concentrations of collectible specimens are the result of millions of years of erosion and weathering that have exposed these minerals at the surface.
tips: Search along the eroded washes and draws on the gentle slopes where winter water flow naturally concentrates loose specimens. Look for translucent or banded pieces showing typical agate coloration (white, gray, orange bands) or jasper's more opaque earthy tones. Bring a small hand rake or brush to clear surface debris and expose fresh material in promising areas. Early visits (March through May) after winter rains bring specimens to the surface, making spring the most productive collecting season.
terrain_notes: Mt. Pisgah stands at 1529 feet elevation with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain is typically open with scattered low vegetation and good visibility across hillsides. Walking distance from parking is generally short (under 1 mile to productive zones). Footing is moderately firm with some loose gravelly material typical of weathered volcanic terrain.
best_season: March - June, September - November. Spring months (March-May) are optimal following winter rains that expose fresh specimens. Fall (September-November) offers pleasant weather and continued good collecting. Summer can be warm and drier; winter brings frequent rain.
written_directions: From Springfield, take Interstate 5 south for approximately 3 miles to the Mt. Pisgah area near Willamette Street. Exit onto Lane County local roads heading east toward Mt. Pisgah Park area. Look for public BLM access points along the foothills near the park boundary. Parking is available at informal pullouts near the base of accessible slopes. GPS coordinates (44.005674, -122.964543) mark the main collecting zone.
road_conditions: Paved roads to area, unpaved pullout access. Light gravel surface typical - passenger vehicles suitable.
parking_notes: Informal pullout parking available near base of collecting slopes. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No formal parking lot - arrive early on weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanized digging. No power tools. Collect only from surface and hand-dug areas. Must stay on BLM land - do not cross onto private adjacent property. Pack out all trash.

nearest_services: Springfield, Oregon is approximately 3-4 miles away with gas stations, restaurants, and water sources. Eugene is 10 miles south with full services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved pullout parking with short walking distance to productive collecting zones. Stable footing on weathered volcanic soil. No wheelchair access to collecting areas, but site is manageable for people with moderate mobility. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for loose gravelly material.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Mt. Pisgah near Springfield?
faq.answer: Mt. Pisgah is known for agate, jasper, quartz, and chalcedony specimens. The agates often display banding in whites, grays, and orange tones, while jasper tends to be more opaque with earthy colors. Most specimens are found loose on the surface or in shallow hand-dug areas along eroded slopes and washes.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Mt. Pisgah in Oregon?
faq.answer: No permit is required at Mt. Pisgah - it is BLM public land open for recreational mineral collecting. You only need to follow the personal use guidelines: maximum 25 pounds per day and no commercial collection. This makes it an excellent no-cost rockhounding destination.

faq.question: Is Mt. Pisgah rockhounding site beginner-friendly and family-safe?
faq.answer: Yes, Mt. Pisgah is highly beginner-friendly with gentle slopes, short walking distances, and abundant surface specimens. Families with children ages 6 and up will find it accessible and productive. Dogs are welcome on BLM land. No technical skills or specialized equipment are required.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Mt. Pisgah Lane County?
faq.answer: Spring (March-May) is optimal because winter rains expose fresh specimens on the surface. Fall (September-November) also offers excellent weather and good collecting. Summer can be warm and drier with fewer exposed specimens. Winter brings frequent rain but can expose new material.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring for rockhounding at Mt. Pisgah?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand rake or brush to clear surface debris, a hand shovel or trowel for light digging, a rock hammer or chisel for specimen extraction, and collection bags. Hand tools only are permitted - no motorized equipment. Wear sturdy hiking boots for loose gravelly terrain and bring water, sunscreen, and a hat.

faq.question: How far is Mt. Pisgah rockhounding site from Springfield Oregon?
faq.answer: Mt. Pisgah is approximately 3 miles from downtown Springfield, making it easily accessible for day trips. The drive takes less than 10 minutes from Springfield. Eugene is about 10 miles south if you need additional services or accommodations.
